evidence: Rectify and startup orchestration layers rely on long, manual `*_WithDefaults` style runtime-merging functions that assign dozens of fields individually instead of using a composable merge/constructor abstraction.

Manual merge code paths are spread across assigned modules, increasing chance of duplicate/missing assignments (a duplicated assignment appears in startup background service defaults wiring, and importer defaults wiring uses repeated boilerplate for every callback field).

The reconcile path in `rectify` emits the same registration transition call twice in one control path, which is consistent with call-path complexity from layered wrappers rather than a single-responsibility transition strategy.

Tests and production files in this batch also reflect heavy state/config parameter threading, confirming that abstractions have become context bags with broad read/write responsibility rather than narrowly modeled dependencies.
abstraction_fitness.issues_preventing_higher_score: The batch contains repeated seam-style default-merging boilerplate and at least one duplicate transition emission side effect, which directly reduces abstraction leverage and adds avoidable indirection risk.

evidence: config/config_update_schema.go defines a generic patch pipeline (ConfPatch + confPatchField + confPatchApplyValueFn) intended to centralize config mutation behavior.

config/urbit.go then exposes the same behavior through six near-identical section-specific wrappers (UpdateUrbitRuntimeConfig, UpdateUrbitNetworkConfig, UpdateUrbitScheduleConfig, UpdateUrbitFeatureConfig, UpdateUrbitWebConfig, UpdateUrbitBackupConfig), each delegating to UpdateUrbit with a section-specific mutate callback.

docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go mirrors these again as RuntimeUrbitOps with separate function fields for each section-specific updater, creating another manual mapping layer between the seam and concrete config mutators.
`goseg/broadcast/loop.go` has a fire-and-forget tick entrypoint (`runBroadcastTick`) that intentionally drops the `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime` error path and returns no signal to callers.

`goseg/routines/version.go` and `goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go` use multiple deferred/explicit close calls without checking returned errors, which creates silent cleanup failures at I/O boundaries that can hide partial-write and shutdown issues.
`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go` also defers `client.Close()` without surfacing possible close failures on the same error-returning path.

findings.summary: Rectify reconcile emits the same transition twice in one reconciliation loop, likely producing duplicate state transitions.
evidence: Inside `StartramRetrieveReconciler.Reconcile` the current-state transition publisher is invoked twice consecutively with identical arguments during the same iteration path.
findings.suggestion: Remove the duplicate publish call and consolidate transition emission to one explicit edge per reconciliation outcome, optionally guarding with a helper that can only publish once per service state transition.

findings.summary: Background startup runtime defaults merge contains duplicate field assignment, indicating fragile hand-maintained wiring.
evidence: `startBackgroundServicesRuntimeWithDefaults` assigns the same override field twice in sequence (`StartDiskUsageWarningFn`), while surrounding default merge logic is manually repeated field-by-field.
The same pattern of copy-forward defaults appears across related reconciler/startup wiring paths, suggesting a brittle abstraction boundary.
findings.suggestion: Run a static consistency pass on all `*_WithDefaults` builders, remove duplicate assignments, and replace repetitive per-field assignment with a shared merge helper that clearly names precedence and preserves explicitness.

findings.summary: Runtime-seam default merging is manually replicated and brittle, with large manual callback-field mapping that offers low abstraction leverage.
evidence: `importerRuntimeWithDefaults` builds an effective runtime by long, repetitive per-field override copies for many dependencies instead of a reusable composition pattern.
Similar `WithDefaults` merge shapes exist in startup initialization/wiring, increasing the shared maintenance burden and making it easy to miss newly added dependencies.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a typed merge helper (or constructor-based defaults) for runtime dependency bundles and apply it across importer/startup/default wiring so adding new seam callbacks is centrally declared and mechanically validated.

findings.summary: Urbit config update seam is split into duplicate section-specific APIs across layers, causing repeated edits and coupling whenever a new section is introduced.
evidence: config/config_update_schema.go already has generic conf patch structures for key-to-field parsing, has-updates checks, and apply/merge behavior.

config/urbit.go hand-maintains separate wrappers per Urbit subsection (UpdateUrbitRuntimeConfig, UpdateUrbitNetworkConfig, UpdateUrbitScheduleConfig, UpdateUrbitFeatureConfig, UpdateUrbitWebConfig, UpdateUrbitBackupConfig).
docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go repeats the same subsection split by exposing six runtime seam callbacks for Urbit section updates.
shipworkflow code uses the section-specific callbacks as control points for mutations, so the same conceptual operation is now represented in multiple API surfaces.
findings.suggestion: Introduce one canonical Urbit config mutation seam (e.g., typed `UpdateUrbitSection(pier, section, mutateFn)` and `UrbitPatch`-style payload) used by config, workflow, and orchestration layers, then generate/compose section-specific convenience adapters only where call sites require ergonomics.

findings.summary: Broadcast tick failures are dropped in the public tick wrapper, breaking consistent error observability
evidence: `goseg/broadcast/loop.go:143` calls `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime(newBroadcastLoopRuntime())` with `_ =`, discarding all returned errors.
`runBroadcastLoop` handles runtime errors from `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime`, but `runBroadcastTick` exposes a no-error API, so callers using this helper never see failures.
findings.suggestion: Either change `runBroadcastTick` to return `error` and propagate it to all callers, or make the wrapper explicitly log and increment a dedicated failure metric so errors are observable and test-assertable.

findings.summary: Cleanup errors are dropped in multiple runtime paths, losing failure signal and weakening diagnostics
evidence: `goseg/routines/version.go` defers `resp.Body.Close()`, `file.Close()`, and `provenanceFile.Close()` without checking returned errors in binary/provenance update paths.
`goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go` defers `conn.Close()`, `cli.Close()`, and `out.Close()` without checking close errors; write failure path also closes without checking the final close result.
`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go` defers `client.Close()` in `disconnect` without inspecting potential failure.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared close-policy helper (for example, `closeQuietly` + `closeWithErr`) and return or aggregate close errors in these functions so transient I/O or client-shutdown failures are not silently lost. For critical updates, fail the operation on close/writeback failure or record an explicit warning with context.
